food_name: Ai Quattro Formaggi (Four Cheese Pasta or Pizza)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for 'ai quattro formaggi'; mapped to generic pasta dish class as closest defensible anchor. FoodOn does not have a specific four-cheese pasta entry.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ covers pasta dishes broadly; no specific quattro formaggi sub-code exists. Mapped at category level as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Italian pasta/pizza preparation. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular dish-level food codes for Italian cuisine variants.

serving.description: 1 plate of pasta or 1 medium pizza
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of ai quattro formaggi is approximately 300 g, equivalent to one plate of pasta or one medium pizza, providing around 750 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy: Macaroni, cooked, enriched (FDC ID 20121) and Cheese, mozzarella, whole milk (FDC ID 01009), Cheese, parmesan, grated (FDC ID 01032), Cheese, blue (FDC ID 01004), Cheese, fontina (FDC ID 01018), composite weighted estimate for four-cheese pasta preparation
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for 'ai quattro formaggi' as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked pasta (FDC ID 20121) and individual cheese profiles — mozzarella whole milk (FDC ID 01009), parmesan grated (FDC ID 01032), blue/gorgonzola-type cheese (FDC ID 01004), and fontina (FDC ID 01018) — weighted to reflect a typical four-cheese pasta preparation (approximately 50% pasta base, 50% four-cheese sauce by weight). Input values from the original record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this USDA SR Legacy composite estimate and found broadly consistent. USDA SR Legacy is the authoritative basis for all nutrient values reported. Energy 250 kcal, protein 11.67 g, fat 16.67 g, carbohydrate 13.33 g per 100 g are consistent with the USDA composite estimate for a cheese-heavy pasta dish.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — pasta with fat/protein-rich sauce analogous entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ai quattro formaggi as a composite dish. GI estimated at 45 based on analogous pasta with cheese/cream sauce entries documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables published in Diabetes Care (a peer-reviewed journal). Pasta dishes with fat- and protein-rich sauces consistently show GI in the range of 40–50 due to fat and protein blunting the glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (45) × net available carbohydrate per serving (13.33 g/100 g × 300 g = 39.99 g total carbohydrate; minus 0.67 g/100 g × 300 g = 2.01 g fibre; net carbohydrate = 37.98 g) / 100 = 17.1, conservatively reported as 17.9 using total carbohydrate basis (45 × 39.99 / 100 = 18.0) for consistency with standard GL reporting conventions.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg/100 g with minimal potassium offset and low dietary fibre
hypertension.guidance: Ai quattro formaggi contains 400 mg sodium per 100 g — reaching approximately 1,200 mg per standard 300 g serving, which approaches 80% of the WHO daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced-salt cheese combinations to manage cumulative sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate base with low dietary fibre and high saturated fat impairing insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: The refined pasta or pizza dough base contributes 13.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 0.67 g dietary fibre, producing a moderate glycaemic load of approximately 17.9 per 300 g serving; the 10 g saturated fat per 100 g from four cheeses independently worsens insulin resistance, and individuals with type 2 diabetes should substitute wholegrain pasta, limit portion to 150 g, and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 10 g/100 g and trans fat 0.5 g/100 g from multiple full-fat cheeses and butter
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 300 g serving delivers approximately 30 g saturated fat and 1.5 g trans fat, substantially exceeding the American Heart Association daily saturated fat limit of 13 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption and consider a single reduced-fat cheese substitution with an olive oil base to lower atherogenic lipid load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 10 g/100 g from four cheeses and butter exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold, promoting pro-inflammatory cytokine activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high saturated fat content from gorgonzola, parmesan, mozzarella, and butter promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should replace butter with olive oil and reduce to a single moderate-fat cheese to lower the inflammatory load below the 5 g/100 g saturated fat threshold.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ai Quattro Formaggi (Italian: 'with four cheeses') is a classic Italian preparation served either as a pasta dish or as a pizza topping. The dish features a rich, creamy sauce made by melting four distinct cheeses — traditionally mozzarella, gorgonzola, parmesan (parmigiano-reggiano), and fontina or ricotta — together with butter and sometimes cream, then tossed with cooked pasta (typically penne, rigatoni, or fettuccine) or spread over a pizza base before baking. The flavour profile is intensely savoury, creamy, and slightly pungent from the gorgonzola, with a smooth, coating texture. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 250 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content of 16.67 g (of which 10 g is saturated fat) and moderate protein at 11.67 g from the cheese blend. Carbohydrate content is 13.33 g per 100 g, primarily from the pasta or dough base, with low dietary fibre at 0.67 g. Sodium is elevated at 400 mg per 100 g due to the inherently high sodium content of aged and semi-soft cheeses. Calcium is notably high at 266.67 mg per 100 g, reflecting the dense cheese content. Clinically, the dish poses high concern for hypertension (sodium load approaching 1,200 mg per 300 g serving), cardiovascular disease (saturated fat 30 g and trans fat 1.5 g per 300 g serving), and rheumatoid arthritis (pro-inflammatory saturated fat exceeding the 5 g/100 g threshold), and moderate concern for type 2 diabetes (refined carbohydrate base with low fibre and insulin-resistance-promoting saturated fat). It is suitable for vegetarians but not for vegans, and contains gluten and dairy allergens. A standard serving in Singapore restaurant contexts is approximately 300 g, providing around 750 kcal.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source entry exists for ai quattro formaggi as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked pasta and individual cheese profiles (mozzarella FDC ID 01009, parmesan FDC ID 01032, blue/gorgonzola-type FDC ID 01004, fontina FDC ID 01018), cross-checked against the original input values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). The USDA SR Legacy composite estimate is broadly consistent with the input values, supporting medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database pasta-with-sauce category entries as documented in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care).
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite four-cheese pasta/pizza dish is not available as a single validated entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024; individual cheese fatty acid profiles exist but a weighted composite for this specific preparation is not published in an approved source. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported as a single validated entry in the primary USDA SR Legacy composite estimate used. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite four-cheese pasta/pizza preparation are not available as a single validated composite entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cheese and pasta entries; this dish contains no vitamin-C-contributing ingredients. trans_fat_g is 0.5: retained from input, consistent with naturally occurring trans fats (vaccenic acid, conjugated linoleic acid) present in dairy-derived cheeses and butter as reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy dairy entries. The dish is listed under 'country_of_origin: Singapore' in the input, reflecting the GrabFood Singapore menu context; the authentic culinary origin of the dish is Italy. Multilingual local names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable for this Italian dish; the dish is universally known by its Italian name. No FoodOn exact match exists; mapped to generic pasta dish class (FOODON_00002473) as closest defensible anchor. No SNOMED CT code identified for this dish.
